# Transcrição: 03-Thinking Like the Algorithm

- **Fonte:** 03-Thinking Like the Algorithm
- **Duração:** 06:35
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:05]

Hey, guys, so let's have a little bit of a chat around the Facebook algorithm because if you can... if you can think like the Facebook algorithm or if you understand how it thinks, then essentially you can improve the results that you're getting from your campaigns. So it's important to know that when you launch ads on Facebook, you are entering inside of an auction against hundreds of thousands, millions of other advertisers vying for users on Facebook, right? The attention of users or the eyeballs, I should say. And inside of this auction process, what will essentially determine how well you compete against other advertisers and therefore the results that you get, how cheap your clicks are, how cheap your conversions come in at, etc., etc., will come down to a couple factors that Facebook takes into account. And basically this is your bid. So it considers the amount that you're willing to bid inside of that auction process. It considers the estimated action, so the likelihood of people to convert or download your freebie or purchase whatever it is you're trying to sell. And then it also takes into the relevance and the ad quality. So this also factors in the quality score of the ad account as well. So the relevance is, well, how... if you're talking about barbecues in your ads but you're sending people to a landing page about beach towels, you may be losing the scent here or if you're targeting doctors but you're showing them an ad about beach towels, again, it may not be extremely relevant, right? So this equation here is essentially what allows you to compete with guys like Coca-Cola. Because even though they have a massive, massive ad budget, Facebook, the number one priority of Facebook is to protect the user experience, right? If it wants more engaged people, because the more engaged people are, the more advertisers will want to spend on that platform, etc., etc. So that's its number one priority. And it basically tries to ensure that the ads that it shows to its users are going to be relevant, are good quality ads. And if yours is not, it will penalize you. Therefore it will cost you a lot more money in the auction process and it could even be a situation where you don't even spend your budget. It stalls completely. Now when you're running conversion-based objective campaigns, which most of you will be, it also takes into account the conversion rate of your landing page. So it looks at how many hits am I sending you, how much traffic am I sending you versus

## [03:05 - 06:07]

how many people are converting on the other side of this. If it turns out that that's quite a low number, then Facebook will also penalize you for that, because it's thinking to yourself, to itself, hey listen, I'm sending you some traffic here, but people are not opting in. I don't think your offer is very good. I don't think your funnel is very good. So I'm going to show the ads of your competitors to this audience instead, and I'm going to show your ads to lower quality audience at this stage, because I'm here to protect the user experience. So it's important that you understand that the conversion rate also comes into, also gets factored into that equation. So typically, and what Facebook says on face value, is that it wants to see about 50 conversions per ad set per week if you're using the default seven-day conversion window, which most of you will be. Now this means that if, let's say you're optimizing a conversion campaign for purchases for sales, what this means is that Facebook is telling you, hey, I want to see about 50 sales per ad set per week. Now you might be thinking to yourself, holy crap, like that's a lot of sales. I don't think I'm even going to get close to that. And if that's the case, knowing the way the algorithm works, one of the things that a lot of advertisers will do is actually optimize for the step beforehand in their funnel, which may be getting a lot more triggers. For example, the step that happens before a purchase is normally add to cart, right? So you would optimize for add to cart instead of purchase, because you'd be getting a lot more of those than a purchase event. And so you would be helping Facebook to learn faster and to optimize the activities better and to also ensure that you're competing properly inside of that auction process. So if you are running campaigns and you've had some really great results, but then things start to putter out, things are not working as well, your CPMs are rising. One of the things that you would look at here is what is the conversion rate of my landing page? Am I getting 50 event triggers per ad set per week? And if the answer is no to that, do I need to optimize for a step beforehand in my funnel so that I can compete and get back on the real playing field inside this auction process? So understanding how the algorithm works is going to really benefit you later on, understanding that your advertiser bid, how much you're willing to bid, and that manual bidding, changing your bid amount can help you compete, understanding that your conversion rate has a big impact,

## [06:07 - 06:35]

and then also understanding the estimated action, how likely your audience is to consume what you have to offer is also going to play a big part. So this is the algorithm here, guys. And if you ever get stuck or your campaigns don't perform well, this is probably the first place you need to turn to is look at each one of these factors that go in the auction process and evaluate if you are actually performing well with these things.
